content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


June 18, 1993

Theresa A. Canady
Director of Client Services
Psomas and Associates
3901 Lime Street
Riverside, California 92501

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
    Our File No. I-93-206

Dear Ms. Canady:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your
advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision,
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to
evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.

QUESTIONS

     1.     As planning commissioner for the County of Riverside,
may you process entitlements for clients of your employer with the
county staff, including the planning department, and represent the
clients before the County Board of Supervisors?

     2.     May you participate in planning commission decisions
concerning clients represented by your employer?

     3.     May your employer, an engineering firm, continue to do
civil engineering work for the County of Riverside as one of the
county's approved engineers?


_________________________
     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Divi-
sion 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

     ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-93-206
Page 2


CONCLUSION

1. The Act prohibits a public official who has a financial
interest in a decision before the official's agency from
attempting to use the official's position to influence that
decision. Therefore, you may not appear before your own agency
(the planning commission) or contact county planning staff to
represent a client regarding a matter which is, or may come,
before your own agency. However, generally speaking, a public
official can represent the interests of a client before an agency
not under the budgetary or appointive control of the official's
own agency.

2. You may participate in decisions concerning applicants
represented by your employer, provided the decisions will not have
a material financial effect on your employer or you personally.

3. The Act does not prohibit your employer from continuing
to do civil engineering work for the County of Riverside.
Furthermore, your employer is not prohibited from representing
applicants before the planning commission or other county
agencies. However, as an employee of the engineering firm you are
disqualified from participating in decisions before the planning
commission which will have a reasonably foreseeable and material
financial effect on your employer, or any other of your financial
interests. Furthermore, you should request advice from your
county counsel regarding the effect of Section 1090 on the
contracts in question.

FACTS

You have been appointed planning commissioner for the fifth
district in the County of Riverside. The county's planning
commission operates on a district basis. Therefore, each
commissioner has jurisdiction over his or her own district.
Normally, the commissioner in whose district a project is located
makes a recommendation on the project, but the planning commission
may overturn a commissioner's recommendation.

You are also Director of Client Services for Psomas and
Associates, a civil engineering/land surveying firm with offices
in Santa Monica, Costa Mesa, Sacramento, and Riverside. The
engineering firm is on the county's list of approved engineers for
contract work. You are employed in the Riverside office.

You receive salary from Psomas and Associates, which is your
sole source of income. According to my telephone conversation
with you of June 10, 1993, while you were previously entitled to
receive 1/2 percent of the net profit from your employer's
Riverside office, you are no longer eligible to this benefit and
you have never received any distribution from any of the net
profits. Thus, your salary constitutes your entire compensation
from your employer.

Our File No. I-93-206
Page 3


In your capacity as director of client services, you are
responsible for entitlement processing for clients who contract
for that specific service with your firm. Entitlements are not
processed automatically; the scope of the work must be detailed in
a contract and requires separate price schedules. You are also
responsible for business development for the firm.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act (the "Act"), was enacted by the
people of the State of California by initiative in 1974. The
purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act was
to ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed,
would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias
caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests
of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. As a planning commissioner for the County of
Riverside, you are a public official as defined in the Act.
(Section 82048.)

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on:

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu-
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(c) and (d).

For purposes of our analysis, we assume Psomas and Associates
is a business entity. (Section 82005.) Thus, Psomas and
Associates is an economic interest to you because you are an
employee of the firm and because presumably the firm
has been a source of income to you of over $250 in the last 12
months. Thus, you are required to disqualify yourself from any
planning commission decision where it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect on Psomas

Our File No. I-93-206
Page 4


and Associates, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether an effect is material, depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision. For example, where an economic
interest such as a source of income is directly before the
planning commission as an applicant or is the subject of a
decision, the effect of the decision on the source of income is
deemed material and disqualification is required. (Regulation
18702.1(a) (copy enclosed); Combs Advice Letter, No. A-89-177.)

Pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, a person or business entity
is directly before the planning commission when that person or
entity, either personally or by an agent, initiates the proceeding
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or is
a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding. A person or
business entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of
any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with,
the subject person or business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Where a source of income which is a business entity is not
directly before the planning commission, but may be indirectly
affected, Regulation 18702.2 would apply (copy enclosed).

Processing Entitlements

You ask in your letter whether you may continue to process
entitlements for your employer's clients with the county staff,
including the planning department, the building and safety
departments and other departments, to take an application through
the process. You also ask whether you may appear before the
County Board of Supervisors in your private capacity.

An official is disqualified from participating in a decision
which will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect on a financial interest of the official. Disqualification
means that the official cannot participate in discussions, vote on
a matter, or otherwise try to influence members of his or her own
agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18700.1; Levinger Advice
Letter, No. I-88-328; West Advice Letter, No. A-88-413.)

Our File No. I-93-206
Page 5


Prohibited "attempts to influence a decision" include
contacting any member, officer, employee of the official's agency
or any agency under the appointive or budgetary control of the
official's agency. Regulation 18700.1 provides in relevant part:

     (a)  With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an official's agency or
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is at-
tempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or
appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency.  Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to, appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer.

                         * * * *

     (c)  With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an agency not covered by
subsection (a), the official is attempting to use
his or her official position to influence the deci-
sion if, for the purpose of influencing the deci-
sion, the official acts or purports to act on
behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency.  Such actions include, but
are not limited to the use of official stationery.

     Regulation 18700.1(a) and (c).

     Pursuant to subdivision (a), you may not appear before your
own agency³ (the planning commission) or contact the county
planning staff to represent a client regarding a matter which is,
or may come, before the planning commission.  Appearing before the
planning commission would be an improper attempt to use your
official position to influence a decision.  This would include
representation of a client before the planning director when the
decision is one which is appealable to, and may be reconsidered
by, the planning commission.  The prohibition applies because in
both these situations, the decision to be made is "within or

___________________________
     3  Section 82041 defines "local government agency" as a county,
city, or district of any kind including "any department, division,
bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the
foregoing."  Generally, the Commission has treated different
governmental departments as different agencies.  For example, a
planning commission is a distinct agency from the city council or
the department of city planning.  (Stout Advice Letter,
No. I-88-313.)

Our File No. I-93-206
Page 6


before" the public official's agency. (Cauble Advice Letter,
No. I-90-427.)4 This restriction would also apply when you
represent clients who are submitting applications in other
districts within the county other than the district you represent
as planning commissioner because you would then be representing clients
before your agency, the planning commission.

However, pursuant to Regulation 18700.1(c), as long as you do
not purport to represent the planning commission,5 you are not
restricted in representing clients before other county agencies,
or agencies outside of the county, which are not under your
agency's budgetary control in your capacity as an employee of the
engineering firm. This would include, for example, any Board of
Supervisors hearing where a client has a project in your
district, or outside of the district you represent in the planning
commission.

Employer's Representation

You also ask in your letter whether you may vote on planning
commission decisions where your employer will come before the
planning commission to represent the firm's clients. In analyzing
similar situations, we have concluded that where a source of
income appears before a planning commission in a representative
capacity, as an agent of an unrelated business entity, and the
decision applies to an economic interest which is an economic
interest of the unrelated business entity, the source of income is
not appearing directly before the planning commission. (Gill
Advice Letter, No. A-85-252.) Thus, where Psomas and Associates
is not the subject of a proceeding before the planning commission,
but would be merely representing the subject of the proceeding,
your employer would not be directly involved in the proceeding and
consequently, your disqualification would not automatically be
required.

However, under these circumstances, you must still disqualify
yourself where your employer will be indirectly materially

4 However, in the Cauble Advice Letter, supra the Commission
advised that if the director of planning of a city and his or her
department of planning are not under the appointive or budgetary
control of the planning commission, a planning commissioner may
appear before the director of planning to represent a client
provided both of the following conditions are met: (1) the
decision is one which is appealable directly to the city council
and, (2) the official does not purport to act on behalf of the
planning commission.

5 Purporting to represent the planning commission would include
using official stationery to communicate with the planning
director or any other member, officer, employee or consultant of
the county planning department. (Cauble Advice Letter, supra.)

Our File No. I-93-206
Page 7


affected.  Whether an effect on a business entity is material is
determined by examining the magnitude of the foreseeable financial
effect on the source of income pursuant to Regulation 18702.2.
Regulation 18702.2 provides that whether the indirect effect on a
business entity is material depends on the financial size of the
business entity.  You have not provided us any facts with respect
to the size of your employer's firm.  However, for a relatively
small business entity, the indirect effect of a decision is
material where:

     (1)  The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

     (2)  The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

     (3)  The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

     You should consult Regulation 18702.2 to determine whether
this is the applicable standard for determining materiality
relating to your employer and a particular governmental decision.
Disqualification would be required where the indirect financial
effect on the firm will be material.  For example, where the
client's payment to the firm for representation was somehow
contingent on the planning commission's approval of the client's
application, the planning commission's decision could materially
financially affect the firm to the extent set forth in Regulation
18702.2.  (Schenk Advice Letter, No. I-90-460.)  Absent facts
indicating a foreseeable financial impact on Psomas and Associates
or you personally as a result of a decision, you may participate
in decisions concerning applicants represented by your employer.

     You also ask in your letter whether you may vote on projects
submitted on behalf of clients of another office within your
employer's firm.  For example, if Kaufman and Broad is doing
business with your Sacramento Office, you ask if you may vote on a
project of Kaufman and Broad in Riverside if the project is
unrelated to the Sacramento project.  It would appear that the
analysis discussed above would apply with respect to a decision
before the Riverside County Planning Commission, even where the
same client has two unrelated projects within your firm.

     Furthermore, you are not restricted from participating in
governmental decisions which will affect other projects of clients
of your employer, unless the effect on the client will
foreseeably result in a material financial effect on you or your

Our File No. I-93-206
Page 8


employer.  (Regulation 18702.2; Roalman Advice Letter,
No. A-91-248 .)

Employer's Work with the County

     You also ask whether your employer's firm can continue to do
civil engineering work for the County of Riverside as one of the
county's approved engineers. The Act does not prohibit your
employer from continuing to do civil engineering work for the
County of Riverside.

     Furthermore, as noted above, your employer is not prohibited
from representing applicants before the planning commission or
other county agencies.  However, as an employee of the firm you
are disqualified from participating in decisions before the
planning commission which will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on your employer, or any other of your
financial interests.

     We also recommend that you contact your county counsel's
office regarding the effect of Section 1090 on the contracts in
question.  Section 1090 is not within the Commission's
jurisdiction.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Jeff Marschner
                                        General Counsel
                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Luisá Menchaca
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

determined by examining the magnitude of the foreseeable financial
effect on the source of income pursuant to Regulation 18702.2.
Regulation 18702.2 provides that whether the indirect effect on a
business entity is material depends on the financial size of the
business entity.
You have not provided us any facts with respect
to the size of your employer’s firm.
However, for a relatively
small business entity, the indirect effect of a decision is
material where:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3)
The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.
You should consult Regulation 18702.2 to determine whether
this is the applicable standard for determining materiality
relating to your employer and a particular governmental decision.
Disqualification would be required where the indirect financial
effect on the firm will be material.
For example, where the
client’s payment to the firm for representation was somehow
contingent on the planning commission’s approval of the client’s
application, the planning commission’s decision could materially
financially affect the firm to the extent set forth in Regulation
18702.2.
(Schenk Advice Letter,
No.
1-90—460.)
Absent facts
indicating a foreseeable financial impact on Psomas and Associates
or you personally as a result of a decision,
you may participate
in decisions concerning applicants represented by your employer.
You also ask in your letter whether you may vote on projects
submitted on behalf of clients of another office within your
employer’s firm.
For example, if Kaufman and Broad is doing
business with your Sacramento Office,
you ask if you may vote on a
project of Kaufman and Broad in Riverside if the project is
unrelated to the Sacramento project.
It would appear that the
analysis discussed above would apply with respect to a decision
before the Riverside County Planning Commission,
even where the
same client has two unrelated projects within your firm.
Furthermore, you are not restricted from participating in
governmental decisions which will affect other projects of the
clients of your employer, unless the effect on the client will
foreseeably result in a material financial effect on you or your


Our File No.
1—93-206
employer.
(Regulation 18702.2;
Roalman Advice Letter,
No.
A—91—248 .)
Employer’s Work with the County
You also ask whether your employer’s firm can continue to do
civil engineering work for the County of Riverside as one of the
county’s approved engineers.
The Act does not prohibit your
employer from continuing to do civil engineering work for the
County of Riverside.
Furthermore,
as noted above, your employer is not prohibited
from representing applicants before the planning commission or
other county agencies.
However,
as an employee of the firm you
are disqualified from participating in decisions before the
planning commission which will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on your employer,
or any other of your
financial interests.
We also recommend that you contact your county counsel’s
office regarding the effect of Section 1090 on the contracts in
question.
Section 1090 is not within the Commission’s
jurisdiction.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission June 18, 1993 Theresa A. Canady Director of Client Services Psomas and Associates 3901 Lime Street Riverside, California 92501 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-93-206 Dear Ms. Canady: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. QUESTIONS 1. As planning commissioner for the County of Riverside, may you process entitlements for clients of your employer with the county staff, including the planning department, and represent the clients before the County Board of Supervisors? 2. May you participate in planning commission decisions concerning clients represented by your employer? 3. May your employer, an engineering firm, continue to do civil engineering work for the County of Riverside as one of the county's approved engineers? _________________________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Divi- sion 6 of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Sec- tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. I-93-206 Page 2 CONCLUSION 1. The Act prohibits a public official who has a financial interest in a decision before the official's agency from attempting to use the official's position to influence that decision. Therefore, you may not appear before your own agency (the planning commission) or contact county planning staff to represent a client regarding a matter which is, or may come, before your own agency. However, generally speaking, a public official can represent the interests of a client before an agency not under the budgetary or appointive control of the official's own agency. 2. You may participate in decisions concerning applicants represented by your employer, provided the decisions will not have a material financial effect on your employer or you personally. 3. The Act does not prohibit your employer from continuing to do civil engineering work for the County of Riverside. Furthermore, your employer is not prohibited from representing applicants before the planning commission or other county agencies. However, as an employee of the engineering firm you are disqualified from participating in decisions before the planning commission which will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your employer, or any other of your financial interests. Furthermore, you should request advice from your county counsel regarding the effect of Section 1090 on the contracts in question. FACTS You have been appointed planning commissioner for the fifth district in the
